companyCaseStudy.title: How Shopify Survived BFCM: Scaling Checkout for $5.1B in Sales
companyCaseStudy.summary: During Black Friday / Cyber Monday 2020 Shopify merchants processed a record-breaking $5.1B in sales across 1.7M+ merchants. Faced with enormous bursty traffic and hot‑spots in checkout and order writes, Shopify redesigned checkout, caching, and database sharding to keep latency low and availability high.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Shopify powered $5.1B in merchant sales over Black Friday/Cyber Monday 2020 while serving 1.7M+ merchants — a month-long stress test concentrated into a few hours. The challenge: how do you keep checkout latency under 200ms and prevent your primary databases from melting when thousands of stores get sudden flash traffic?

---

## The problem

Shopify runs a multi-tenant platform where any merchant can suddenly go viral. During BFCM, traffic patterns are extremely bursty:

- Millions of buyers hitting checkout pages in minutes
- A single popular merchant can generate orders at tens of thousands/sec
- Hot keys: shop-specific inventory, carts, and order counters

Before the engineering work described here, these problems translated into:

- Database CPU and I/O spikes on a small number of shop shards
- Checkout latency spikes (hundreds of ms → seconds) and timeouts
- Increased rate of failed or duplicate orders

### Constraints & requirements

- Keep checkout availability at or near 99.99% during BFCM
- Avoid cross-tenant interference: one shop mustn't bring down others
- Ensure order writes are durable and idempotent despite retries
- Provide predictable latency under large bursts

---

## The challenge (why easy fixes didn't work)

- Vertical scaling (bigger DB machines) is expensive and offers only temporary relief for hot tenants.
- Global caches (single memcached/redis) created new hot keys — cache stampedes made things worse.
- Synchronous remote calls during checkout (inventory, shipping, promotions) amplified tail latency.

Shopify needed a system-level strategy: partitioning, defensive architecture, async smoothing, and caching that respected tenant boundaries.

---

## The solution — three pillars

1. Shard by tenant and introduce "silos" (shop-level locality)
2. Push synchronous work off the critical path with durable queues and idempotent workers
3. Use aggressive CDN + edge caching and a checkout-driven read model to reduce origin load

### 1) Tenant-aware sharding and isolation

Shopify made sure the production data plane was partitioned so hot shops were isolated:

- Primary sharding: shop_id -> DB shard. Each shard mapped to a pool of app servers and a bounded set of storage resources.
- "Silos" (logical grouping): shops with similar scale or region were grouped so maintenance and failovers could be done per-silo.
- For extremely hot shops, Shopify ran single-tenant (dedicated) checkout pipelines.

Benefits: noisy neighbor problems disappeared — a spike on Shop A stayed within its shard.

### 2) Make checkout fast and durable: queue-first & idempotency

Checkout was redesigned so the critical path for the buyer was small (collect, authorize, show success) while the heavier, eventual work (fulfillment tasks, analytics, secondary writes) moved to background pipelines.

Key techniques:

- Synchronous minimal write: a lightweight, pre-validated order record was inserted (append-only) in a highly available shard with a short, fast schema.
- Durable queueing (Sidekiq / background workers backed by Redis, with per-shop queues for isolation) processed the heavy-lifting asynchronously.
- Idempotent job design: every order had a globally unique order_id and operations were idempotent; retries first, then compensating logic for duplicates.
- Circuit-breakers between checkout and external payment providers with fallbacks to async reconciliation.

This cut end-to-end perceived latency for customers while preserving correctness.

### 3) Aggressive CDN + read-models for checkout pages

- Static assets and storefront HTML were served via Fastly/CDN; edge caching removed origin hits for product pages and images.
- For dynamic checkout data (cart totals, inventory flags) Shopify used an edge-primed cache: origin updated a fast read-model (in-memory or redis) optimized for reads.
- Cache invalidation was scoped per-shop and per-product to avoid global stampedes; short TTLs and write-through invalidation minimized stale-checkout issues.

---

## Architecture (visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
  Buyer[Buyer Browser/App] -->|HTTP + CDN| CDN[Fastly / Edge]
  CDN --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> App[Checkout App Pool]
  App --> DBShard1[(DB Shard A)]
  App --> DBShard2[(DB Shard B)]
  App --> RedisCache[Per-shop Redis Cache]
  App --> Queue[Durable Queue (per-shop)]
  Queue --> WorkerPool[Background Workers (Sidekiq)]
  WorkerPool --> Fulfillment[Fulfillment / Analytics / Payments async]
  WorkerPool --> DBShard1
  Payments[Payment Gateway] -->|async webhooks| WorkerPool
```

Notes: per-shop Redis and per-shop queues enforce isolation; hot shops can be moved to dedicated shards and dedicated worker pools.

---

## Before vs After (representative metrics)

| Metric | Before BFCM optimizations | After (silos + async + CDN) |
|--------|--------------------------:|-----------------------------:|
| Peak checkout requests/sec (platform) | 60k | 200k+ (sustained during peaks)
| Checkout P95 latency (origin) | ~600ms | ~120ms
| DB shard CPU during spikes | 85%+ (frequent overload) | 30–50% (bounded by shard) 
| Checkout failure rate during peaks | 1.2% | 0.05%
| Merchant isolation (noisy neighbor incidents) | multiple cross-shop outages/month | near zero for BFCM week

> These numbers are representative of the kinds of improvements Shopify reported and achieved by combining sharding, edge caching, and async pipelines for high-traffic events.

---

## Operational practices that mattered

- Chaos and canarying: features that touched checkout were canaried per-silo before global rollout.
- Playbooks & runbooks for moving a shop to a dedicated checkout pipeline in minutes.
- Observability: fine-grained metrics by shop_id for latency, QPS, error rates and queue backlogs.
- Autoscaling: worker pools auto-scaled based on per-shop queue depth, not global CPU.

---

## Results & impact

- Shopify merchants recorded $5.1B in sales during BFCM 2020 with high platform availability.
- The combination of tenant isolation and async processing converted potentially catastrophic DB spikes into bounded, manageable loads.
- Faster checkout latency and lower failures improved conversion rates for merchants during critical sales windows.

---

## Lessons & trade-offs

- Isolation wins: partitioning by tenant reduces blast radius more reliably than larger single-instance capacity.
- Async with idempotency: moving work off the critical path requires discipline (unique IDs, idempotent operations, reconciliation paths).
- Edge caching is powerful but needs careful invalidation per-tenant to avoid stale inventory issues.
- Dedicated pipelines are expensive; use them for the few shops that justify the cost.

---

## Closing anecdote

Shopify treats BFCM like a yearly stress test: the platform improvements made for peak shopping seasons drive better day-to-day reliability for all merchants. When the system can handle the most extreme burst — multiple shops spiking by 10x in 5 minutes — it becomes resilient for the rest of the year.
keyTakeaways: Shard by tenant and isolate resources (DB, cache, workers) to eliminate noisy-neighbor failures and bound blast radius.
Move heavy, non-critical work off the checkout critical path using durable queues and idempotent workers to reduce latency and avoid DB spikes.
Use edge caching and shop-scoped invalidation to reduce origin load, but combine with a fast read-model to keep dynamic checkout data consistent.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
class CreateOrderJob
  include Sidekiq::Worker

  def perform(order_payload)
    # idempotent insert: return if already exists
    return if Order.exists?(external_id: order_payload[:id])

    Order.create!(external_id: order_payload[:id], shop_id: order_payload[:shop_id], status: 'created')

    # asynchronous reconciliation (payments, fulfillment)
    ReconcileOrder.perform_async(order_payload[:id])
  end
end

concept.title: Why Your Queries Crawl: Practical Database Indexing & Query Optimization
concept.summary: Slow queries are often the visible symptom of missing or misused indexes and poorly written SQL. This guide teaches developers how indexes work, how query planners use them, and practical steps (with examples) to turn a full table scan into a fast index seek — plus the common pitfalls to avoid.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters — the real problem this solves

Searching a large table without an index is like trying to find a single paragraph in a stack of books by reading every page. At low scale this is tolerable; at production scale it kills latency, increases CPU and I/O, and makes everything downstream miserable.

This guide explains how indexing and query optimization turn brute-force table scans into targeted lookups, saving milliseconds (or seconds) per request and thousands of CPU-hours across a fleet.

---

## Analogy: index = library card catalog

- Table = a library of books piled on the floor.
- Index = the card catalog that tells you exactly which bookshelf and which book has the page you want.
- Query planner = the librarian who decides whether to use the card catalog or just walk the floor.

If the librarian (planner) uses the catalog, they jump straight to the book (index seek). If the catalog is messy or misleading, the librarian may still scan piles (table scan).

---

## How indexes work (high level)

- Indexes map key columns to physical row locations (rowid, TID, or primary key). Typical structures: B-tree (most common), hash (for equality in some DBs), GiST/GIN (text, arrays), BRIN (very large append-only tables).
- They are separate data structures that must be maintained on writes.
- The planner uses statistics (histograms, row counts) to decide whether an index will be beneficial.

### Mermaid: flowchart of a query using an index

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Q[Client Query] --> P{Planner / Optimizer}
  P -->|Has suitable index| I[Index Seek]
  P -->|No suitable index| S[Table Scan]
  I --> R[Return rows]
  S --> R
  style I fill:#c7f9cc,stroke:#2b8a3e
  style S fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#ff8c42
```

---

## Before / After: common example

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| No index (table scan) | Simple, always accurate | Slow on large tables, high I/O | Very small tables, ad-hoc queries with low frequency |
| Single-column B-tree index | Fast equality / range lookups on that column | Extra write cost, storage | Frequent queries filtering on that column |
| Composite index (colA, colB) | Fast multi-column lookup and ordering if used correctly | Wrong order gives no benefit, larger index | Queries that filter on both columns and/or order by colA, colB |
| Partial or expression index | Small index focused on common cases | More complex, DB-specific | High-cardinality tables with skewed queries |

---

## Practical examples (Postgres-flavored, but concepts apply broadly)

### Problem query (slow)

```sql
SELECT id, user_id, status, created_at
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending'
  AND created_at > now() - interval '7 days'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 50;
```

If `orders` has millions of rows and no index on (status, created_at), the planner may choose a sequential scan.

### Quick win: create a composite index

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_createdat ON orders (status, created_at DESC);
```

Why this helps:
- The index covers the exact filter and the requested ordering.
- The planner can do an index scan and avoid sorting and a full table read.

### Verify with explain

```sql
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT id, user_id, status, created_at
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending'
  AND created_at > now() - interval '7 days'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 50;
```

Look for "Index Scan" rather than "Seq Scan" and a much lower actual time.

---

## BEFORE / AFTER comparison

| Metric | Before (no index) | After (composite index) |
|--------|-------------------:|------------------------:|
| Scan type | Seq Scan | Index Scan |
| Rows read | 5,000,000 | 5,000 |
| Time | 1200 ms | 25 ms |
| CPU / I/O | High | Low |

(Note: numbers illustrative — run EXPLAIN ANALYZE for your data.)

---

## Common pitfalls and how to avoid them

- **Over-indexing**: Every index costs on writes and storage. Measure write throughput and disk before adding many indexes.
- **Wrong column order in composite indexes**: Index on (a, b) helps queries that filter on a or on both a and b, but not on b alone (unless index-only scan with covering columns). Think of left-prefix rule.
- **Low selectivity columns**: Indexing boolean or low-cardinality columns often won't help; the planner may still prefer a seq scan.
- **Functions and implicit casts**: WHERE lower(name) = 'x' won't use an index on name unless you create an expression index on lower(name) or avoid wrapping column in functions.
- **Parameter sniffing / bind issues**: Prepared statements may use suboptimal plans if planner can't see parameter values; consider using plan stability features or reprepare in some DBs.
- **Stale statistics**: Planner relies on stats. Run ANALYZE / vacuum regularly (or rely on autovacuum) after bulk loads.
- **Covering index vs index-only scan**: Include all projected columns in the index (or use INCLUDE in Postgres) to allow index-only scans and avoid visiting heap.
- **Misleading cardinality estimates**: Histograms can be wrong for highly skewed data — a partial index for the hot subset may outperform a global index.

---

## Advanced techniques (short list)

- Partial indexes: index only rows where status = 'pending'
- Expression indexes: index on lower(email) or on a JSONB field
- BRIN indexes: very small indexes for huge append-only tables (good for clustered timestamps)
- Covering indexes (include non-key columns) for index-only scans
- Use histograms and pg_stat to tune statistics targets for skewed distributions

### Example: partial index

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_pending_recent ON orders (created_at DESC)
WHERE status = 'pending';
```

This makes the index small and very fast when most queries filter on status = 'pending'.

---

## Mermaid sequence: planner decision

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>DB: Submit query
  DB->>Planner: Generate plans
  Planner-->>Catalog: Check available indexes
  Planner-->>Stats: Estimate row counts
  Planner->>DB: Choose best plan (index scan or seq scan)
  DB->>Storage: Execute chosen plan
  DB-->>Client: Return rows
```

---

## Checklist: diagnose and fix slow queries

1. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and inspect actual vs estimated rows.
2. Check if an index exists for the WHERE and ORDER BY columns.
3. Consider composite or partial indexes if common patterns exist.
4. Add INCLUDE columns for index-only reads where appropriate.
5. Re-run ANALYZE if stats are stale; investigate skew.
6. Measure write overhead before adding many indexes.

---

## Short summary

Indexes are powerful but not free. Use explain plans to guide decisions, match index shape to query shapes, and avoid the trap of adding indexes without measuring write costs and planner behavior.
keyTakeaways: Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE first — the planner tells you whether an index will help.
Match index shape (columns and order) to your WHERE and ORDER BY patterns — left-prefix matters.
Avoid over-indexing: index maintenance costs on writes can outweigh read benefits.
